coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Ti3SiC2 from powder blend of Ti, Si and TiC
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hashimoto, Hitoshi
Sun, Zheng Ming
Inoue, Yosuke
Tada, Shuji
coredata.dc:description: Titanium silicon carbide Ti3SiC2 was synthesized from a powder blend of Ti, Si and TiC at a molar ratio of Ti:Si:TiC=2:2:3. The powder blend was pressed and shaped into a cylinder and then sintered at various temperatures in vacuum. X-ray diffraction analysis was conducted on the cylinders sintered at various temperatures. Results of the XRD analysis revealed that Ti5Si3, the most thermodynamically stable phase in Ti–Si system, was preferentially formed by the reaction between Ti and Si powders followed by the formation of TiSi2 from remaining Ti and Si powders in a relatively low temperature range. These silicides reacted with TiC to form Ti3SiC2 at higher temperatures. The final phases in the cylinders sintered at temperatures over 1729K were Ti3SiC2 and Ti5Si3, which well agrees with those estimated by a Ti–Si–C phase diagram. On the other hand, final phases in disks sintered under pressures of 14MPa or more by using a carbon mold were found to be Ti3SiC2 and TiC, which suggests the equilibrium changes with the pressure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ceramics
Solid-state reactions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for sintering experiment of cylindrical powder compact in vacuum by a pulse discharge sintering machine.
Illustrated configuration of holes made on cylindrical powder compact for temperature measurement by infrared thermometer and thermocouple (cross-sectional view).
Relation between reading of infrared thermometer and that of thermocouple.
X-ray diffraction patterns of powder compact sintered in vacuum at 1307, 1412, 1518, 1570, 1623 and 1729K.
Variation of relative intensity of each main peak from Ti, Si, TiC, Ti5Si3, TiSi2 and Ti3SiC2 with sintering temperature.
Composition of starting powder blend plotted on a Ti–Si–C ternary phase diagram at 1500K.
Variation of relative intensity of each main peak from TiC, Ti5Si3, TiSi2 and Ti3SiC2 with sintering temperature.
Variation of relative intensity of each main peak from TiC, Ti5Si3, TiSi2 and Ti3SiC2 with sintering pressure.
Variation of volume fraction of Ti3SiC2 in the sintered disc and density of the disc with sintering pressure.
Variation of relative intensity of each main peak from Si, TiC, Ti5Si3, TiSi2 and Ti3SiC2 with sintering temperature.

article.ce:title: Synthesis of Ti3SiC2 from powder blend of Ti, Si and TiC

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Research Institute for Sustainable Development, National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology, Shimoshidami, Moriyama-ku, Nagoya 463-8560, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Student of Aichi Institute of Technology, Yagusa-cho, Toyota 470-0392, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Titanium silicon carbide Ti3SiC2 was synthesized from a powder blend of Ti, Si and TiC at a molar ratio of Ti:Si:TiC=2:2:3. The powder blend was pressed and shaped into a cylinder and then sintered at various temperatures in vacuum. X-ray diffraction analysis was conducted on the cylinders sintered at various temperatures. Results of the XRD analysis revealed that Ti5Si3, the most thermodynamically stable phase in Ti–Si system, was preferentially formed by the reaction between Ti and Si powders followed by the formation of TiSi2 from remaining Ti and Si powders in a relatively low temperature range. These silicides reacted with TiC to form Ti3SiC2 at higher temperatures. The final phases in the cylinders sintered at temperatures over 1729K were Ti3SiC2 and Ti5Si3, which well agrees with those estimated by a Ti–Si–C phase diagram. On the other hand, final phases in disks sintered under pressures of 14MPa or more by using a carbon mold were found to be Ti3SiC2 and TiC, which suggests the equilibrium changes with the pressure.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium silicon carbide, Ti3SiC2 shows a relatively high elastic modulus (320GPa) and oxidation resistance (parabolic rate constant of 2×10−8 kg2 m−4 s−1 at 1273K) as a ceramic material, while it shows an electrical conductivity (4.5×106 Ω−1 m−1) and a thermal conductivity (43Wm−1 K−1) both about two times larger than pure Ti, and an excellent thermal shock resistance (bending strength is not affected by quenching from 1400°C into water) and the machinability comparable to graphite [1]. As these attractive properties arise from its crystal structure itself, the fine control of microstructure is not required, unlike most advanced materials.

Ti3SiC2 had been synthesized by Jeitschko and Nowotny [2] in 1966 for the first time by heating a powder blend of TiH2, Si and graphite up to 2000°C in a graphite capsule. Since then, Ti3SiC2 has been synthesized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [3], arc-melting [4] and solid-state reaction of powder blends [5–9]. Goto and Hirai [3] successfully synthesized almost pure polycrystalline Ti3SiC2 by CVD. However the size was very small (40mm in length, 12mm in width and 0.4mm in thickness). Only the solid-state reaction of powder blends can offer a practical synthetic method for bulk Ti3SiC2. However, this method mostly produces a considerable amount of TiC or Ti–Si binary phases as byproducts. According to Lis et al. [6], the best result was the formation of 82vol.% of T3SiC2, with at least 18vol.% of TiC, by the solid-state reaction of elemental Ti/Si/C powder blend of stoichiometric composition using self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) or combination of SHS and hot isostatic pressing (HIP). Barsoum and El-Raghy [1] succeeded in synthesizing Ti3SiC2 with purity more than 99vol.% by hot press or HIP of a Ti/SiC/C powder blend of the stoichiometric composition, which led to a breakthrough in the practical use of this material.

The authors [10] also succeeded in synthesizing Ti3SiC2 with the purity more than 98vol.% by pulse discharge sintering (PDS) of a powder blend of Ti/Si/TiC. In the solid-state reaction of the elemental Ti/Si/C powder blend, we thought, Ti–Si binary phases and TiC were formed and then Ti3SiC2 was formed by the reaction between these phases. According to the binary phase diagrams [11], TiC has a wide compositional range on Ti-rich side (Ti: 51.2–68at.%). Therefore, Ti-rich TiC was formed by the reaction between the elemental Ti and C, which caused the shortage of Ti for the formation of Ti3SiC2 and therefore the residue of TiC. So we got an idea that use of a commercial TiC powder having a very narrow compositional range as one of the starting materials instead of C powder can avoid the shortage of Ti for the formation of Ti3SiC2. We investigated the composition of a powder blend of Ti/Si/TiC for the formation of pure Ti3SiC2 and found that a nonstoichiometric composition (Ti:Si:TiC=2:2:3 in molar ratio) made Ti3SiC2 of 98vol.% or more.

In this report, the reaction route and the final phases were investigated in the solid-state reaction of Ti/Si/TiC powder blend with the nonstoichiometric compositions in order to obtain the basis of pure Ti3SiC2 synthesis.

Commercially available Ti powder (99.9% pure, 10μm in particle size), Si powder (99.9%, −10μm) and TiC powder (99%, 2 or 5μm) were used in this experiment. A chemical analysis was done on the TiC powder because that TiC has the wide compositional range as mentioned above. The result showed that the TiC powder contained 79.6wt.% of Ti and 19.6wt.% of C, corresponding to TiC0.99. These powders were blended at a molar ratio of Ti:Si:TiC=2:2:3 using a Turbular mixer for 86.4ks. A 7-g powder blend was pressed under a pressure of 200MPa and shaped into a cylinder of 14mm in diameter and 17mm in height using a die set and a press machine. A hole of 5mm in diameter and 7.5mm in depth was made at the axial center of the cylindrical powder compact for temperature measurement by an infrared thermometer. The powder compact was heated in vacuum using a carbon mold and a pulse discharge sintering machine (Sodick PAS-V) as shown in Fig. 1 . The powder compact was insulated electrically using alumina plates put on both ends of it. The compact was fixed with an upper carbon punch put on it but no load was applied to the upper punch. The load was applied to carbon blocks on the carbon mold to keep electrical contact between electrodes of the sintering machine and the carbon mold. Thus the electric current passed only through the blocks and the mold and heated them. The powder compact was heated from outside by the mold under a very small pressure (The load applied to the powder compact was merely the weight of the upper punch.). The temperature of the powder compact was measured by the infrared thermometer focused on bottom of the hole made on the compact. Before experiment, the thermometer was calibrated for the powder compact because the thermometer had been already adjusted to carbon. The calibration was conducted by using the compact on which another hole for the temperature measurement by a K-type thermocouple was bored at right angle to the hole for the thermometer (see Fig. 2 ). The compact was heated in the same way as shown in Fig. 1 and the temperature was measured by the thermometer and thermocouple. The result is shown in Fig. 3 . The thermometer indicates lower temperatures than the thermocouple but a straight line can express the relation between two readings. The calibration of thermometer was done in a temperatures range below 1423K because that a stainless steel sheath of the thermocouple is likely to react with the powder compact at temperatures over 1423K. Temperatures measured by the thermometer were corrected using the straight line extrapolated to higher temperatures over 1423K, and the temperatures described in the following part of this paper are the corrected values.

The powder compact was heated in vacuum at a rate of 0.33K/s up to 1307, 1412, 1518, 1570, 1623, 1729, 1834 and 1940K, kept for 900s and cooled in the sintering machine. For comparison, a 17-g powder blend was sintered under a pressure of 50MPa at 1373, 1473, 1573, 1623 and 1673K by using a carbon mold having a 30-mm inner diameter. The temperature was measured by the thermometer focused on the bottom of a hole (5mm in diameter and 10mm in depth) made on the carbon mold at its axial center. The heating rate and the period kept at the maximum temperature were the same as the sintering of powder compact. As the sintered disc had reacted with the carbon mold to form TiC near the surface of sintered disc when sintering at temperatures of 1623 and 1673K in a preparative experiment, thin graphite sheets were put between the powder blend and the carbon mold to prevent the reaction between the disc and mold. The sintered disc reacted with carbon mold was so hard due to TiC formed near the surface that polishing was very difficult. The use of graphite sheets successfully prevented the reaction and the polishing of sintered disc was easy.
X-ray diffraction analysis was made on polished ends of the sintered compacts and discs by using Philips X’pert MPD with Cu Kα radiation to identify the phases.

X-ray diffraction patterns of the compacts sintered at 1307, 1412, 1518, 1570, 1623 and 1729K are shown in Fig. 4 . Peaks from the starting powders (Ti, Si and TiC) are seen together with peaks from Ti5Si3 when sintering at 1307K. The peaks from Ti and Si disappear at 1412K and peaks from TiSi2 appear. In a temperature range from 1518 to 1623K, a rapid increase in the intensity of peaks from Ti3SiC2 is seen, while the intensity of peaks from TiC, Ti5Si3 and TiSi2 decreases rapidly. To illustrate the phase change with sintering temperature, relative intensity of each main peak from Ti, Si, TiC, Ti5Si3, TiSi2 and Ti3SiC2 is plotted against the temperature in Fig. 5 . To analyze the reaction route exactly, the in situ phase analysis during reaction is required by using synchrotron radiation or neutron radiation. However, we can estimate the reaction route roughly from this figure as follows: (1) Ti5Si3 was formed by the reaction between the elemental Ti and Si and then the formation of TiSi2 started. According to Riley et al. [12], the formation of Ti5Si3 is preceded by the α/β transformation in Ti, which initiates interdiffusion of Ti and Si, in the self-propagating high-temperature synthesis of Ti5Si3 from a powder blend of Ti and Si. As the α/β transformation occurs at 1138K [11], it is reasonable that Ti5Si3 was already formed at 1307K. (2) The formation of these titanium silicides was terminated until 1412K because of the complete consumption of the elemental Ti and Si. It should be noted that we blended the elemental Ti and Si powders at an equimolar ratio (Ti:Si:TiC=2:2:3). However, TiSi was not formed but Ti5Si3 and TiSi2 were formed. In Ti–Si system, there are five intermetallic compounds, Ti3Si, Ti5Si3, Ti5Si4, TiSi and TiSi2 and among them Ti5Si3 is the most thermodynamically stable phase with the highest formation enthalpy (ΔH =−579kJ/mol) [12]. It is presumed that the most stable phase, Ti5Si3 was formed primarily by the reaction between Ti and Si. TiSi and TiSi2 have almost the same formation enthalpy, 130 and 135kJ/mol, respectively [13]. Therefore, the shortage of the elemental Ti powder in the starting blend caused by the formation of Ti5Si3 resulted in the preferential formation of Si-rich compound, TiSi2. (3) The formation of Ti3SiC2 by the reaction between these silicides and TiC started at 1518K and grew rapidly in the range from 1518 to 1623K, which resulted in the rapid decrease in TiC and the silicides. (4) The formation of Ti3SiC2 was almost terminated until 1729K and two phases, Ti3SiC2 and Ti5Si3 remained until 1940K as the final products.

The composition of starting powder blend is plotted on a Ti–Si–C ternary phase diagram section at 1500K [14] by symbol “X” in Fig. 6 . The starting composition lies in a three-phase region, Ti3SiC2–TiC–Ti5Si3, very close to an immediate two-phase region, Ti3SiC2–Ti5Si3. Therefore the final products well agree with those estimated by the phase equilibrium diagram.

On the other hand, the final products in the powder blend sintered under the pressure of 50MPa by using the carbon mold were Ti3SiC2 and TiC as shown in Fig. 7 . The final product TiC could arise from the reaction between the sintered disc and the graphite sheets. So the sintered disc was polished more than 1mm and XRD pattern was measured again. As the XRD pattern did not change, we believed that the final product TiC did not arise from the reaction between the sintered disc and the graphite sheets. By comparing Fig. 7 with Fig. 5, the reaction route under 50MPa seems to be the same as that under 0MPa. That is, Ti5Si3 was formed by the reaction between the elemental Ti and Si and then the formation of TiSi2 started. Then Ti3SiC2 was formed by the reaction between these silicides and TiC. Therefore, the reaction route was not so affected by the pressure, while the final products depended on the pressure.

Therefore, the effect of sintering pressure on the products in sintered discs was investigated. The powder blend was sintered at 1673K for 900s under a range of pressures from 0 to 60MPa by using the carbon mold. The variation of relative intensity from each main peak from Ti3SiC2, TiC, Ti5Si3 and TiSi2 with sintering pressure is shown in Fig. 8 . Ti3SiC2, TiC, Ti5Si3 and TiSi2 were seen in the disc sintered under 0MPa, while only Ti3SiC2 and TiC remained under 14MPa or more. Thus Ti5Si3 tends to disappear under pressure, while TiC tends to remain. Fig. 9 shows the variation of volume fraction of Ti3SiC2 in the sintered disc and density of the disc with sintering pressure. The volume fraction was calculated by the intensity of Ti3SiC2 and TiC XRD peaks using the following formula [15]:
(1)
V a
= K / ( K +
I b
/
I a
)
where V a is the fraction of Ti3SiC2, I a and I b the integrated intensity of Ti3SiC2 (104) peak and TiC (200) peak, respectively, and K is a constant having values of 1.8 for the calculation of weight fraction and 1.95 for the volume fraction. As this formula is applicable to composites consisting of Ti3SiC2 and TiC only, the volume fraction could not be calculated for the disc sintered under 0MPa. From the figure, both the density and the volume fraction seem to be almost independent on the sintering pressure in a pressure range over 14MPa. More detailed investigation on the pressure effect should be done in the future.

According to Wu et al. [16] who investigated the reaction route in the pressureless reactive sintering of a stoichiometric powder blend of Ti/SiC/C by using neutron powder diffraction technique, intermediate phases TiC x and Ti5Si3C x (x ≤1) were formed first. As C is soluble in Ti5Si3 up to about 10at.% in the ternary equilibrium phase diagram [14], Ti5Si3C x is the same as Ti5Si3 in this case. These phases were consumed in the formation of Ti3SiC2 and TiC x remained. Thus Ti3SiC2 is formed by the reaction between Ti5Si3 and TiC and the final products tend to be Ti5SiC2 and TiC for the stoichiometric powder blend. So we prepared a stoichiometric powder blend of Ti/Si/TiC and sintered in vacuum under 0MPa. Fig. 10 shows the variation of relative intensity of each main peak from Si, TiC, Ti5Si3, TiSi2 and Ti3SiC2 with sintering temperature. The reaction route estimated from this figure is the same as the nonstoichiometric powder blend. However, the final products are Ti3SiC2 and TiC. The volume fraction of Ti3SiC2 calculated from the intensity of Ti3SiC2 and TiC XRD peaks is 0.97 for 1729K or more. The stoichiometric powder blend produces Ti3SiC2 with a small amount of TiC as the final products regardless of starting powder combination.

Ti3SiC2 was synthesized from the powder blend of Ti, Si and TiC at the molar ratio of Ti:Si:TiC=2:2:3. From the result of XRD analysis on the compacted powder blend sintered at various temperatures in vacuum, the reaction route was estimated. As a result, Ti5Si3 was formed by the reaction between Ti and Si and then the formation of TiSi2 started. These titanium silicides reacted with TiC to form Ti3SiC2 in the next stage. The final phases in the powder compacts sintered at temperatures over 1729K were Ti3SiC2 and Ti5Si3, which well agrees with those estimated by a Ti–Si–C phase diagram. On the other hand, the final phases in the disks sintered under pressures of 14MPa or more by using a carbon mold were Ti3SiC2 and TiC, which suggests the equilibrium changes with pressure.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: ASM's Binary Alloy Phase Diagrams, 2nd ed., Plus Updates on CD-ROM, Ver. 1.0, ASM International, 1996.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Handbook of Ternary Alloy Phase Diagrams on CD-ROM, Ver. 1.0
